# Transcrição: 11-Deal Structures

- **Fonte:** 11-Deal Structures
- **Duração:** 05:37
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Welcome back. In this next lesson, we're going to discuss different deal structures. Traditionally, there are three ways of structuring acquisition deals. Equity acquisition, asset acquisition, and merger. In this course, we're going to primarily cover asset acquisitions, but I'll cover the other two as well. With an asset acquisition, you're not purchasing the business, the assets themselves, and the buyer can choose which assets and which liabilities that they want to assume when they buy the business. Those assets could be machinery, real estate, buildings, vehicles, IPs, the business's name, whatever is identified in the purchase agreement. So at the end of the deal, the selling company continues to operate as an entity with whatever remaining unsold assets are left. In many cases, the asset acquisition strategy is the best deal structure for sellers looking for cash transactions. Or this strategy works very well for acquiring assets from bankrupt companies. Okay, next we'll talk briefly about mergers. In the case of mergers, you have two or more companies coming together forming one brand new entity. You'll probably recognize some relatively recent mergers such as Heinz and Kraft becoming the Kraft Heinz company. There's a couple ways mergers can be set up. There are purchase mergers where one company purchases another company. The purchase is made with cash or through the issue of some kind of debt instrument. The sale is taxable, which attracts the acquiring companies who enjoy the tax benefits. Acquired assets can be written up and identified with the actual purchase price. And the difference between the book value and the purchase price of the assets can depreciate annually, reducing taxes payable by the acquiring company. Another option is a consolidation merger. With this merger, a brand new company is formed and both companies are bought and combined under the new entity. The tax terms are the same as those of a purchase merger. Mergers aren't really the scope of what we teach in this course. So let's move on to equity acquisitions. In equity acquisitions, you're buying the equity or the shares of the business you want to acquire. You can't buy a business as an individual. You need to set up something called a shell company. Shell companies exist only on paper and have no office and no employees, but might have a bank account or be the registered owner of assets such as a business. This is something that your contingent fee lawyer can create for you or buy it for you. Then that will be the vehicle you use to purchase the company. The trading company will be 100% subsidiary of the shell company. The company will most likely be an LLC or a limited liability company. Your contingency fee lawyer will advise you on what the best vehicle is for you. And what you choose will depend on the state you're located in, the

## [03:03 - 05:37]

type of deal you're doing, your personal income taxes, the tax advantages, the protection that you want to enjoy, all these things, but they will definitely figure that out for you. Most of the time, it's going to be something as simple as an LLC. If you're not targeting an equity-based deal, and instead you want to do an asset-based deal, you'll essentially be starting a brand new company. It's essentially a startup. You'll still have the assets, the customers, any employees that stick around, etc. But what you won't have is the past credit history that an LLC will have. And we need the credit history for the financing aspect of the acquisition. If you don't have that, you have to set up a new bank account and get the paperwork started. So a shell company will keep the target business intact under ownership of you and the shareholders. There are many factors to consider in an asset acquisition. The buyer only acquires the assets and liabilities that it identifies and agrees to acquire and assume, subject to any liabilities imposed on the buyer as a matter of law or condition. This is fundamentally different from an equity acquisition or merger where the buyer acquires all the assets and liabilities of the target company. This is why I don't actually recommend an equity purchase because you're not certain what all those liabilities could be. The ability to pick or choose specific assets and liabilities gives you the buyer more flexibility. That way you're not wasting money or unwanted time on assets where there's more risk or undisclosed liabilities. However, this also makes asset acquisitions a bit more complex because the buyer has to spend time identifying the assets and liabilities it wishes to acquire and assume. Again, I generally recommend an asset acquisition instead of equity acquisitions. But you should consult your deal team to ensure you're using the best deal structure for your deal, especially if you're buying something that has tax losses or an asset that you can use in future operations that you would lose that benefit if you did an asset purchase versus a stock purchase. Understanding the type of purchase you're doing, the purpose you're doing it for and what's involved in it is absolutely critical. So once again, this is where I stress having the right professionals will make or break a great deal. Okay, that's it for our lesson on deal structures. See you in the next lesson.
